text: Robotic repair of traumatic posterior diaphragmatic hernia. The objective of this presentation is to provide the learner with the operative approach and technique for robotic repair of a posterior diaphragmatic hernia. Our patient is a forty-one year old female. She has a past medical history significant for motor vehicle collision in two thousand seven from what she suffered multiple broken ribs among other orthopedic injuries on the left side. In two thousand eighteen she presented with a cold with persistent cough. She underwent a chest x-ray, which showed infiltrate in the left lower lobe and was treated for pneumonia. Follow-up chest x-rays did not show resolution of the pneumonia, leading to a CT of the chest. She has a large posterior left diaphragmatic defect. The diaphragm was largely detached posteriorly from the ribs. The spleen, stomach, and large bowel appear to be within the defect.

The patient is positioned in steep reverse Trendelenburg in the right lateral decubitus position. A 12mm robotic trocar is placed in the left hemiabdomen at the midclavicular line, just lateral to the semilunar line. An 8mm robotic camera with a 30-degree angle is inserted into the intra-abdominal cavity. Two additional 8mm trocars are inserted along the left hemiabdomen just below the costal margin, and an assistant port is placed in the subxiphoid location to accommodate the air seal.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon identifies a defect in the posterior left diaphragm, with the colon, omentum, and spleen visible within the chest cavity. The stomach is also identified and carefully reduced from the chest back into the abdomen. The surgeon uses electric cautery to remove adhesions from the diaphragm and free the hernia edges circumferentially. The defect, measuring five by seven centimeters, is closed using O-tychron interrupted mattress sutures, with barred felt cut into one by two centimeter rectangles used as pledges.

A seven French drain is placed into the thoracic cavity before the defect is completely closed. The most posterior aspect of the defect is sutured close to the ribs using three separate interrupted sutures, which are pulled through the abdominal wall fascia and tied externally. A stab incision is made subxiphoid, and an endoclose device is used to pull up the free limb of the suture. The exteriorized suture is secured with a clamp, and the endoclose is passed again through the same incision at an angle, incorporating about one centimeter of abdominal wall between the sutures. The suture is then tightened to create tension on the defect and tied down.

A 15x20 cm dual-sided barred mesh with an echo positioning system is selected to reinforce the defect closure. The left lobe of the liver is mobilized, retracting its attachments to the midline, and the peritoneal reflection of the descending colon at the splenic flexure is mobilized to accommodate the mesh. The mesh is introduced into the abdominal cavity through the twelfth port and centered on the most posterior aspect of the defect. It is secured to the diaphragm and abdominal wall circumferentially with 2-0 V-lock sutures, and the central portion is fixated to the diaphragm closure using the same sutures.

Here is the finalized appearance of the hernia repair. Post-operatively, a chest x-ray is taken on post-op day one that shows a well-positioned Blake drain with full inflation of the left lung and no sign of failure of the repair. Blake drain was removed on post-op day two and the patient is discharged on the same day. The patient is seen in follow-up two weeks later with no further issues.
